input.draft: Thank you so much for the kind words — we'll share this with the whole team!

review.text: Wonderful practice, everyone was so friendly and gentle. Highly recommend!

notes: Issue #72 example 1. Textbook safe reply: thanks, no care-relationship confirmation, no specifics.

input.draft: We're sorry to hear about your experience. We'd love the chance to make it right — please call our office and ask for our practice manager.

review.text: Really disappointed with how my appointment went. Felt rushed and dismissed.

notes: Issue #72 example 2. 'Your experience' acknowledges the feedback without confirming care; invites the private channel — the canonical negative-review pattern.

input.draft: Thank you for the feedback. We take every comment seriously and are always working to improve.

review.text: Mediocre experience, front desk could be friendlier.

notes: Issue #72 example 3. Generic acknowledgment, nothing to disclose.

input.draft: We appreciate you taking the time to leave a review.

review.text: Fine visit overall.

notes: Issue #72 example 4. Minimal and safe.

input.draft: We're sorry things fell short of your expectations. We can't discuss details here, but our team would welcome a call.

review.text: Two hours in the waiting room and then a surprise charge. Not going back.

notes: Issue #72 example 5. Explicitly declines to discuss details in public — exactly the right move.

input.draft: Thanks for the 5 stars!

review.text: Great!

notes: Issue #72 example 6. The bare digit must not trip the date/time/phone regexes.

input.draft: We strive to make every visit comfortable, and we're glad that came through.

review.text: Always a calm, comfortable experience here.

notes: Issue #72 example 7. 'Every visit' is generic — the care-context rule only fires on second-person possessives ('your visit').

input.draft: We're sorry your root canal on March 3rd was uncomfortable.

review.text: My root canal here was the most painful experience of my life.

notes: Issue #72 example 8. Procedure tied to the reviewer ('your root canal') + explicit date + care-relationship confirmation. Deterministic layer alone must block this — no model required.

input.draft: Your bill was $450 because your insurance denied the claim.

review.text: Charged way more than I was quoted. Feels like a scam.

notes: Issue #72 example 9. Dollar amount + 'your insurance' + 'your bill'. Deterministic block on three separate rules.

input.draft: Our records show you missed two appointments before this visit.

review.text: They refused to see me and blamed me for it. Rude and disorganized.

notes: Issue #72 example 10. No regex-visible disclosure — the block must come from the LLM layer (contradicts_reviewer_privately: disputing the reviewer with information only the practice has).

input.draft: We enjoyed having you as a patient for the last three years.

review.text: Moving away and sad to leave this practice. Three great years!

notes: Issue #72 example 11. Warm, well-meant, and a flat confirmation of a care relationship plus its duration — LLM layer must block (confirms_care_relationship); no deterministic rule sees it.

input.draft: The crown we placed for you carries a five-year warranty.

review.text: My crown cracked after eight months. Unacceptable for what I paid.

notes: Issue #72 example 12. 'The crown we placed for you' — the possessive follows the term, so the deterministic layer only warns (generic procedure mention); the LLM layer must recognize the care confirmation and block.

input.draft: As we explained at your appointment last Tuesday, the copay is set by your carrier.

review.text: Nobody warned me about the copay. Sneaky billing.

notes: Issue #72 example 13. Relative date ('last Tuesday') + 'your appointment' + 'your carrier' + insurance term. Deterministic block on multiple rules.

input.draft: That's simply not what happened — you were seen on time and declined the treatment we recommended.

review.text: They kept me waiting an hour and never explained my options.

notes: Issue #72 example 14. Contradicts the reviewer using visit details only the practice would have ('you were seen', 'declined the treatment') — LLM block (contradicts_reviewer_privately), with defensive tone on top.

input.draft: We're sorry, but you were quite rude to our front desk staff, which is why the visit went the way it did.

review.text: Front desk treated me like a nuisance from the moment I walked in.

notes: Issue #72 example 15. Defensive, blame-shifting tone — warn, never block: tone problems are not privacy violations (the LLM tone category is clamped to warn server-side).

input.draft: Please call us at 555-0123 to discuss your treatment.

review.text: Still confused about what was done and what I owe.

notes: Issue #72 example 16. Phone number is only a warn (a practice's published number is fine), but 'your treatment' confirms the care relationship — deterministic block.

input.draft: We're sorry — call us and ask about our whitening options.

review.text: Wish they offered more cosmetic services.

notes: Issue #72 example 17. Generic mention of a procedure the practice offers ('our whitening options') with no care link — the first-person possessive marks it as the practice's offering, not the reviewer's care.

input.draft: We've been proudly serving this community for over 20 years, and we're sorry we let you down.

review.text: Expected better from such an established office.

notes: Near-miss for the LLM care-relationship category: a duration about the PRACTICE, not the reviewer's patient history. Must not be confused with 'having you as a patient for three years'.

input.draft: Your feedback helps us improve — thank you for taking the time.

review.text: A few suggestions: better signage, shorter waits.

notes: Near-miss for the deterministic 'your X' rule: 'your feedback' is safe — feedback is not a care-context noun. Guards against over-broad possessive matching.

input.draft: We understand statements can be confusing. Our office manager is happy to walk through any questions privately — please give us a call.

review.text: The statement I got makes no sense at all.

notes: Near-miss for the care-context rule: 'statements' without a second-person possessive is generic; the reply routes billing to a private channel, which is the safe pattern.

input.draft: We're sorry for the confusion — insurance questions are best handled over the phone with our front desk.

review.text: Insurance billing here is a mess.

notes: Generic insurance mention: warn tier (insurance talk belongs in a private channel), not block — nothing ties it to the reviewer's own coverage.

input.draft: You were charged four hundred fifty dollars because the claim was denied.

review.text: Massive surprise bill after what should have been a routine visit.

notes: Spelled-out dollar amount near billing words — the dollar rule must catch amounts written in words, not just '$450'. 'The claim' also draws an insurance warn.

input.draft: Join us on 3/14 for our community open house!

review.text: Love this office, always something going on for the neighborhood.

notes: Deliberate over-block: 3/14 is an event date, not an appointment, but the date rule blocks all explicit dates by design — a false block is a cheap edit, a leaked appointment date is not (issue #72 implementation notes).

input.draft: Our office is open until 5pm on weekdays if you'd like to talk.

review.text: Could never reach anyone by phone.

notes: Clock times warn (not block): office hours are legitimate, but a time tied to the reviewer's visit would disclose appointment details, and the regex can't tell which is which.

input.draft: We're open Saturdays and weekday evenings — please stop by or call whenever works for you.

review.text: Impossible to get an appointment that fits a work schedule.

notes: Near-miss for the date rule: bare plural 'Saturdays' without 'on/every/last' and without a possessive is published-hours talk, not an appointment reference. Guards against the weekday regex over-reaching.

input.draft: No one should ever be surprised by a bill for hundreds of dollars. Please call our office and ask for the practice manager so we can look into this together.

review.text: Got a bill for hundreds more than the estimate. Absurd.

notes: Near-miss for the dollar rule: 'hundreds of dollars' is an order of magnitude, not an amount — no specific figure is disclosed, and the reply routes to a private channel. Guards against the spelled-out-amount regex over-matching.

input.draft: We'd really like to talk this through. Please give us a call at (555) 010-1234 and ask for our office manager.

review.text: No one ever answers the phone at this office.

notes: A bare phone number with no care-context possessive: the phone rule warns (the practice's published number is fine, but the check can't know whose number it is) and nothing upgrades it to block.

input.draft: Thank you for being part of our dental family for the past five years — patients like you make it all worth it!

review.text: Five years with this practice and every visit has been great.

notes: Warm, grateful, and a flat public confirmation that the reviewer has been a patient for five years. No deterministic rule sees it ('dental family' is not a care-context noun) — the LLM layer must block (confirms_care_relationship).

input.draft: Reply right here with exactly what you were charged and we'll go through it line by line in the comments so everyone can see the truth.

review.text: They overcharged me and nobody will explain the charges.

notes: Invites a public back-and-forth about billing specifics instead of a private channel — the LLM invites_public_dispute category (warn). No deterministic rule fires: no amount, no possessive care noun.
